item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't help but feel as if the pun was a large part of the motivation though.

Actually, no. I had written the entire text of the article before the pun title occurred to me.

That being said, I love a good dad joke. Many of my article titles are bad puns: “The Stuff Dreams are Made Of”, “Irrational Root,” “Glory Hole”, “All Those Fucking Ideas”…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is just magical beyond belief. The tone, the theme, the setting…all captured well. I applaud you!

Though yeah, epistolary fiction of the time can be tricky

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like I said in my author commentary, that's pretty much what I was going for. In the late 19th century, epistolary fiction was in vogue. Since this piece is set during that time period, I decided to copy that literary style as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I gotta admit, the whole demonic possession thing kind of bogged it down for me, but the way you kept building the format of Garfield's revival and those involved kind of reminded me of Bram Stoker's Dracula, with all the back and forth notes and evidence. Certainly an upvote.

And THANK GOD for not incorporating post-internet speech.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Author here: I am out of the country and away from computers for a while. Can this one be moved to SCP-4336? (I have a bunch of other articles numbered SCP-??36 and might as well keep that tradition going).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That passage is a word-for-word cut and paste from the actual New York Times article for that day. Blame the original journalist for the word choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough! I always related the word drive in relation to animals in more of a herding context. ie, driving cattle/a cattle drive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The use of the word "drove" here tripped me up a bit. Did they have some early prototype car or something, or did they just call getting into a carriage or whatever "driving"? A minor quibble, but it was something that stuck out to me so I figured I'd mention it.

The word drive is older than cars, and originally referred to piloting a horse drawn carriage or similar. It doesn't necessarily refer to motor vehicles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While this does, quite unfortunately, share a lot of things in common with a number of other contest entries, it's very well written and an enjoyable read. It was a little obvious how the whole thing was going to play out pretty early on, but all of the letters and other documents are so well written that it doesn't really detract from anything.

Everything here feels authentic, and I'm definitely a fan of articles in the "we got this from pre-Foundation people, best not fuck with it" genre, so it's a +1 from me.

ETA: Oh, and kudos for the article title too.

Minor issues
– hide block

Contemporaneous records made at the time that SCP-4000 was placed into containment suggest that SCP-4000

Double use of SCP-4000 makes this read a little clunkily. Suggest replacing the first use of "SCP-4000" here with "it".

President Garfield and Secretary Blaine drove from the Executive Mansion, about 9 o'clock yesterday morning,

The use of the word "drove" here tripped me up a bit. Did they have some early prototype car or something, or did they just call getting into a carriage or whatever "driving"? A minor quibble, but it was something that stuck out to me so I figured I'd mention it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How many contradictory origin stories does the Foundation have? I think we're beating most superheros at this point.

Beyond that, this was quite the ride. I can't help but feel as if the pun was a large part of the motivation though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels the most authentic of all the entries imo. If there was a category for Best Adaptation to a Time-Period's Parlance, this would take the cake by a long shot. Your writing is very impressive. I love the inclusion of the graphophone as a means to record the conversations, very resourceful imagining.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was certainly not a fan of circling back around to another Foundation origin story, but everything else throughout is immaculately constructed and bleeds pure authenticity. I'm willing to overlook a trope I personally have grown sick of due to some overall wonderful execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Echoing Roget on this. The dedication to authenticity really makes this stand out among other contest entries. I love the various formats that combine to tell the narrative through newspaper clippings, recordings, notes, and the like. Exactly what you want to see in a solid history contest entry.

On top of that, the narrative itself is compelling, and the buildup to the eventual possession of President Garfield is quite engaging. The title also got a laugh from me.

+1. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I might have a new favorite? The letters from Bell are absolutely fantastic, some of the best writing, and especially historical writing, I've seen on this site.

SCP-4000 is James Abram Garfield, the 20th President of the United States. The containment of SCP-4000, which pre-dated the formal organization of the Foundation, commenced following an assassination attempt on the then-President.

I will say this should probably be the first paragraph of the description. It's pretty obvious by this point who the object is, so there's no sense in burying it.

Damn, but I really like this one. I'm not salty at all that it's way better than my assassination of Garfield idea

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I replied that I was sorry to find him unhappy that we had taken the bullet out but that we had no intention of putting it back in.

What spunk!

This piece is perhaps just a smidge longer than is necessary, but I still enjoyed it thoroughly. I think part of my enjoyment was just the sheer amount of historiography on display — I don't know how much research you did for this, but the tone and diction for each document was just about pitch-perfect for the period.

One minor glitch:

+ show block
– hide block

GARFIELD:[in a lower voice]] Garfield…Garfield…[sound of man laughing]

You seem to have two ']]' there. I'd have fixed it myself, but at the time I noticed, you were editing the document.

ETA: I fixed the above glitch, and took out your stray [[/div]]s (you had a few extra? They weren't doing anything/were showing up on the page). I also moved one of your [RECORDING ENDS] into the quote-box (as I'm pretty sure it was just a mistake, since the previous one had been in one). Pardon if any of those were intentional (though I can't imagine they were!).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Madame Blavatsky explained that whatever animating spirit had taken residence in President Garfield’s body, it was not at her summoning although she had anticipated that this might happen.

What a liar. And hypocrite. Just because the 6th root race of man won't be her and her pals, she switches sides :p

Edit: it did briefly occur to me that she's not lying, and by helping the spirit into this world, she meant she'd cast some spell to help ensure the body stays alive, or that will bind any old spirit to it but not necessarily Garfield's or Lucifer's. But whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love the dedication to authenticity here, all of this reads like something that could've been ripped straight from the time period. I got a lot of flashbacks to Mary Beard's Destiny of the Republic here, and since that is one of my favorite historical books it's definitely a good thing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reserved for author commentary and image credits.

Commentary: The theme of this article is, for me, a bit of site history. My very first post to the wiki was a SCP write-up of a reanimated zombie of James Garfield. It was terrible and didn't last more than a few hours before deletion (though a fragment did end up here: http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-100-j). I did vow, however, to return to the subject some day. The 4000 contest motivated me to actually follow through.

A word about "I have been called many names…" Yes, it's intended as corny and over-the-top. There are two kinds of period-fidelity that I'm going for in this piece: the first is text-based period-fidelity by which I mean conformity of the epistolary documentation to the style of the time. This is part of my method: if I'm going to write a fictional journal entry by Madame Blavatsky or a fictional letter by Alexander Graham Bell to his wife, first I read examples of authentic documents of that type so that I can try to capture the style and cadence of that sort of writing. The second is narrative period-fidelity. Think of your favorite late-19th-century genre writers: Bram Stoker; H.G. Wells; Jules Verne; Arthur Conan Doyle; Rudyard Kipling … there is in them a somewhat refreshingly shameless boldness - after all, the tropes they were using weren't yet cliches.

Basically everybody in the story is an actual historic character. Bell and his wife Mabel really did have a passing interest in spiritualism and the occult- then again, it seems like basically everybody did in that time period. Crookes was a Theosophist as well as a Royal Fellow. Lucretia Garfield really was a ghost-hunter. In our timeline, Dr. Bliss remained in charge of Garfield's care until Garfield's slow, painful death by infection, and there is no indication that Bell remained part of the team apart from the single-visit attempt to use his apparatus to find the bullet. Bell really did speak Mohawk, though, and was friends with George Johnson.

And Madame Blavatsky wasn't in the United States at the time of the Garfield assassination- she was in Asia. (The words that I put in her mouth are, however, largely hers. She was quite a cantankerous person.)

I didn't initially mean for this to be Yet Another Foundation Origin Story, but there you have it.

Some image links:

  • Everything except for the Visible Speech excerpt is from Wikimedia Commons
  • The Visible Speech excerpt is from Bell's actual letter to his wife in that script, from the Library of Congress collection: https://www.loc.gov/item/magbell.03500108/

Some text credits:

  • Helena Blavatsky's cranky letters to the editor, and the style (but not the content) of her diary entries, comes from http://www.katinkahesselink.net/blavatsky/
  • Specific cited quotations from Blavatsky's published writings come from the cited sources
  • The New York Times excerpt is word-for-word from https://timesmachine.nytimes.com/timesmachine/1881/07/03/issue.html

If you like this sort of thing, here's a link to my author page which will bring you to my other works: http://www.scp-wiki.net/spikebrennan-s-personnel-file
